title: Let's learn about the View Transition API
description: Chrome 111 introduced an interesting feature called the "View Transition API". In this article, we'll take a look at the View Transition API. What...

children.value:
Since the version Chrome 111, we was added an interesting feature called the "View Transition API".
In this article, we'll take a look at the View Transition API.
What is the View Transition API?

The View Transition API provides the ability to easily create animated transitions between different DOM states while updating DOM content at once.
Let's take a look at some examples of where it can be used.

If the order of elements changes due to user interaction, it may be difficult to identify which elements have changed.
In this case, a reordering animation can help the user recognize that the order has changed.
In the example above, the View Transition API is applied to the draggable elements to make the reordering easier to recognize.

props.title: Page transition effects for server-side rendering

children.value:
SSRs that utilize backend frameworks are MPAs, which means that it takes a lot more work to implement page transition animation effects compared to SPAs.
However, by utilizing the View Transition API, it is relatively easy to implement page transition effects.
In the demo video above, we applied the View Transition API to Express + Handlebars to implement the page transition effect, and the video plays uninterrupted even when the page is switched.
💡 As this is still an experimental feature, it only works with Chrome, Edge, and Opera, which are Chromium Engine-based browsers.

For practice, we've created a simple example that toggles the shape of a shape when a button is clicked.
We'll build on this example and add some code to demonstrate how to use it.

The basic usage is really simple.
Just update the DOM inside
and the transition will be applied immediately.

directly on click to updating via the View Transition API.
If you click the button, you can see that the default transition effect, cross-fade, is applied.
The View Transition API can be summarized in the following steps.
is called, the API takes a screenshot of the current page. 2.
is called to change the DOM.
Capture the new state of the changed page as a live representation.
Construct a pseudo-element tree with the following structure.
is the top-level pseudo-element that contains all View Transition pseudo-element overlays.
is a screenshot of the old page we worked on in
is a live representation captured in
Both of these render as content similar to
, so they can be styled with attributes like
The default effect, a cross-fade, is applied to

This time, let's replace the default transition with a different effect.
We want the pre-update elements to disappear as they fade out to the left, and the post-update elements to appear as they fade in from the right.

I hadn't noticed it before, but when I checked the results, I realized that the transition effect was also applied to the header.
It's a little awkward to have transitions applied to the header when you're navigating through a web page.
The previous example pointed out some awkwardness, so let's create two requirements and modify them accordingly.
I don't want to apply a transition to the header before and after a DOM update because it doesn't change.
I want the button to move naturally as the size of the shape changes.
value, it will smoothly transition to the same element before and after the DOM update.
Headers will remain as they are because there is no pre- or post-DOM update call to smoothly transition them.
The button will be repositioned up or down after the DOM update, depending on the size of the shape, so it will be moved to provide a smooth transition.

corresponding to that value.
This makes it possible to implement different effects for different elements because they act as separate overlays.

value must be unique, and if there are duplicate values within a single page, the transition effect will be ignored.
You can also give shapes a unique

This is a quick overview of the View Transition API.
In the next post, we'll apply the View Transition API to an MPA rendered with Express + Handlebars to implement a page transition effect.
Smooth and simple transitions with the View Transitions API

links.text: What is the View Transition API?

title: Let's find out about requestAnimationFrame()
description: In order to implement animation that is difficult to express with CSS alone, JavaScript must be used, and using it without optimization can cause p...

children.value: Before we start, we need to use some terms repeatedly, but it's long, so let's define it as a contraction.

In order to implement animation that is difficult to express with CSS alone, JavaScript must be used, and using it without optimization can cause performance problems.
Prior to the release of
was used as an optimization technique, and since then,
In this article, we will check what problems the

operates without considering the frame, it cannot guarantee when the callback function is called.
Therefore, if it runs over two frames as shown in the figure below, the animation will not be smooth because it misses one frame.
How does 16ms in the figure come from?
If you didn't specifically choose refresh rate and buy it, you would be using a monitor at 60Hz refresh rate, which means that the screen is

.
Therefore, the screen is updated at intervals of 1,000 ms/60 times ≈

is synchronized with the screen scan rate and is executed when the browser is ready to draw the screen.
To be more specific, the callback function written in
is registered in the Animation Frame queue, and all callback functions in this queue are processed at once

.
Accordingly, it is possible to prevent a problem in which a frame is lost because the callback function is started in the middle.

, we made an animation in which each box moves 5 px to the right, a total of 1,000 px, as an example code.
As mentioned earlier, the timer function is not guaranteed when the callback function will be executed, so frame loss may or may not occur enough to be visually confirmed. In this case, it can be confirmed by repeating it several times.

also works within an inactive window or hidden iframe, wasting system resources, resulting in battery life and performance degradation problems.
However, using
saves system resources by stopping work when the window becomes inactive.

To see this for yourself, let's switch the browser to another tab as soon as we run the animation of the example code mentioned above.
If you wait for about 5 seconds and go back, the animation run with the
is already over, but the function run with the

I don't know where I saw it, but I saw a post saying that scroll events occur so much that I need to throttle them with the code above.
Since then, I have applied the throttle unconditionally when using the scroll event, and when I happened to see the
, I realized that I had been mistaken.
occurs when the scrolled screen is rendered

, it is synchronized with the refresh rate.
Therefore, because it
runs at the same speed as
, which is synchronized with the refresh rate, a throttle using

is often unnecessary.
So in what case is
needed? It is necessary when using code that can cause layout thrashing as shown below.
Reflow, forced synchronous layout and layout thrashing
The above figure shows the workflow when rendering, and it is difficult to explain all the steps, so let's just talk about the reflow required for today's topic.

reflow refers to the process of re-performing from the layout step when the geometric value of the DOM element is changed.
As can be seen from the re-performance from the layout stage, it is a costly task because not only the changed elements but also the calculation of other elements must be re-performed.
Therefore, the browser collects tasks that require reflow in the queue to reduce this cost and schedules them for processing in one reflow in the next frame.

However, when trying to process reading and writing geometric values in one frame, the reflow is performed immediately.
This is called
, and a phenomenon in which forced synchronous layout occurs repeatedly, resulting in performance degradation is called
The code mentioned above reads the geometric value of

, resulting in a forced synchronous layout.
Therefore, we will use the features of
described above to separate the read logic that causes the reflow and hand over the remaining computational logic to

as a callback function.
This effectively defends layout thrashing because the
read is processed in the current frame
rest of the operations are collected in the next frame and processed in one time
Thankfully, someone has already made a

with this topic, so I tested it with the developer tool.
It was processed at once to the extent that I wondered if this was right.
Should rAF throttle be applied to scroll event - Korean
Front-end performance optimization: avoid layout thrashing with rAF - Korean

children.text: Reflow, forced synchronous layout and layout thrashing

title: Make animated number counter with Vanilla js. +Vue3
description: I implemented it because I needed to use a animated number counter, but when I saw my code that I implemented, I was very annoyed. Becuase my code...

children.value:
I implemented it because I needed to use a animated number counter, but when I saw my code that I implemented, I was very annoyed.
Becuase my code didn't make sense. So I searched for code made by someone else and managed to find code that satisfies the requirements that
can be duration-specified and can display commas every 3 digits

To put it really simply, the counter we created increases in numbers at the same rate from start to finish,
so we just have to calculate the number increased by the elapsed time and repeat it over and over again until we finish.
To explain it in more detail, if you multiply
(range that to animate the number), and add

(initial value), you can obtain the value that should be shown at the elapsed time.
By repeating these tasks by the recursive function during duration, we can get the animation effect we want.
It's hard just to say it, so let's say you make an animation that counts from 20 to 100 for 4 seconds.
At the end of one second,
, so the value we see is 1/4*80+20 = 40.
Let's look at how the operating principles described above are applied in the code.
(1) When 'timeLapseRate' becomes 1, that is, when time
elapses by the duration we specify
may be greater than 1. In order to prevent this and make calculations easier, make the result value not exceed 1.
was used to achieve the same effect with negative numbers. In addition, in order to respond to the number format,

was used to show it in a number format suitable for each browser language.
In my case, since i set Korean as the browser language, the number format displays a comma every three digits.
(4) Repeat this task to provide animation effects.
I wrote the Vue3 code with the same mechanism because I use Vue for front-end work.

title: Create a custom function similar to timeago.js using Intl RelativeTimeFormat.
description: If you look at Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube, the time when the content was uploaded in the form of relative time such as xx seconds ago and xx m...

children.value:
When you look at Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube, the time when the content was uploaded in the form of relative time such as 'xx seconds ago' and 'xx minutes ago' is displayed.
It can be easily implemented using some libraries.
However, since it can be implemented easily and similarly through the JS built-in API
, I would like to take this opportunity to record the practice.
It was declared in advance by pairing the unit that wanted to be expressed as the relative time with the value converted into ms units.
Cycle the units specified above sequentially to verify that the diff value is divided by 1 or more. If zero comes out, it does not correspond to that unit, so it goes to the next step.
was used to cut the decimal point. Due to the expression structure,
, so the decimal point cut was required because to make number marked close to zero.
(2) Values less than 1 second are filtered by unit==='second' expression and
